页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：Key information
---
Modes of transmission

Inhalation of respiratory droplets and aerosolised particles; deposits of respiratory droplets and particles on mucous membranes

Incubation period

Most commonly 2–5 days (range 1–14 days).

Period of communicability

Infectious period varies based on individual factors and virus variants. May be infectious 1-2 days prior to symptom onset. Asymptomatic spread is documented.

Burden of disease

The burden of severe disease predominantly lies with older adults and those with comorbidities. Children generally experience milder disease.

Funded vaccines

mRNA-CV: Comirnaty (manufacturer Pfizer/BioNTech).

Dose, presentation, route (see sections 5.4.4 and 5.4.5 )

mRNA-CV (30 μg) for ages 12 years and over

- single-dose prefilled syringe - no dilution required

- intramuscular injection

- store at +2° to 8°C, expiry 12 months

mRNA-CV (10 μg) – paediatric for ages 5 to 11 years

- single dose vial (light blue cap) - no dilution required

- intramuscular injection

- store once thawed at +2° to 8°C, expiry 10 weeks

mRNA-CV (3 μg)- paediatric for ages 6 months to 4 years

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：Key information
---
- multidose vial (yellow cap) - dilution required

- intramuscular injection.

- Storage once thawed: undiluted, +2° to 8°C expiry 10 weeks diluted, in vial +2° to 8°C expiry 12 hours or drawn up +2° to 30°C expiry 6 hours

- undiluted, +2° to 8°C expiry 10 weeks

- diluted, in vial +2° to 8°C expiry 12 hours or drawn up +2° to 30°C expiry 6 hours

Funded vaccine indications and schedule (see section 5.5 )

mRNA-CV (30 μg)– for ages 12 years and over

mRNA-CV (10 μg) – for ages 5 to 11 years

- For those with severe immunocompromise from age 5 years (see section 5.5.2 ) give two primary doses, at least 8 weeks apart

- For anyone else previously unvaccinated - give one dose

- For those who are eligible, variant-matched mRNA-CV can be given from 6 months after previous dose (see section 5.5.3 for eligibility and recommended groups)

mRNA-CV (3 μg) – for ages 6 months to 4 years

- For use in infants and young children with severe immunocompromise or complex/multiple health conditions that increase their risk of severe COVID-19 (see section 5.5.4 )

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：Key information
---
- Three doses as a primary course – dose two is given at least 3 weeks after first, then dose three at least 8 weeks later

- Additional doses can be given from 6 months after any COVID-19 primary course (once or twice yearly, clinical discretion can be applied).

Contraindications (see section 5.6.1 )

A history of anaphylaxis to any component or previous dose.

Precautions (see section 5.6.2 )

A definite history of anaphylaxis to any other product is a precaution not contraindication.

Defer vaccination if individual has current cardiac inflammation.

Defer further doses if individual develops myocarditis/pericarditis after any dose of mRNA-CV Seek specialist immunisation advice regarding future COVID-19 vaccination doses.

Potential responses to vaccine (see section 5.7.1 )

Generally mild or moderate: injection site pain, headache, fever, muscle aches, dizziness and nausea, a day or two after vaccination. These responses are more commonly reported after second dose and in younger adults (<55 years). Responses to subsequent doses are generally similar or milder than after the second dose.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：Key information
---
Vaccine effectiveness (see section 5.4.3 )

Effectiveness of variant-matched formulations of mRNA-CV is maintained against severe disease with recommended additional doses, but wanes for mild disease over a period of weeks after each dose.

Public health measures

For current information refer to www.tewhatuora.govt.nz/for-the-health-sector/covid-19-information-for-health-professionals/

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.1. Virology
---
Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus-2 (SARS-CoV-2) is a member of the Coronaviridae family and the Betacoronavirus genus. This enveloped, positive-strand RNA virus encodes four major structural proteins – spike (S), membrane (M), envelope (E) and a helical nucleocapsid (N). To enter host cells, the spike protein, which forms the characteristic crown-like (Latin: corona) surface structures, binds to the angiotensin-converting enzyme-2 (ACE2) receptor most frequently found on human respiratory tract epithelium.[ 1, 2 ] SARS-CoV-2 was first detected in late 2019 in China and identified as the cause of an acute respiratory illness later known as Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19).

As with most RNA viruses, mutations occur and variant strains of SARS-CoV-2 continually evolve, which can increase transmissibility, alter virulence, or evade immunity. WHO classified genetic variants into three classes: variants of concern, variants of interest and variants under monitoring.[ 3 ]

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.2. Clinical features > 5.2. Clinical features
---
Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID 19) is caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus, which infects the respiratory tract and is transmitted human to human primarily through respiratory droplets and aerosols. Documented transmission has also occurred through direct contact and rarely fomites (objects or materials that can carry infection).

Symptoms of COVID-19 illness range widely from a mild acute respiratory illness to a severe respiratory tract infection with pneumonia, which can lead to severe inflammatory disease and respiratory failure. The infection can also be asymptomatic. Even early in the pandemic when testing was frequent, up to 50 percent of those with confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection were asymptomatic.[ 4 ] For most people COVID-19 is a mild disease, particularly since most have immunity from previous infection and immunisation, but some develop more severe disease or exacerbation of comorbidities.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.2. Clinical features > 5.2. Clinical features
---
The usual symptoms of COVID-19 resemble other common respiratory illnesses, and include a new or worsening dry cough, sneezing and rhinorrhoea or nasal congestion, fever, sore throat, shortness of breath and fatigue. A distinctive feature of COVID-19 is a temporary loss of smell or altered sense of taste. Some cases have gastrointestinal symptoms including nausea, diarrhoea, vomiting and abdominal pain. Headache, muscle aches, malaise, chest pain, joint pain, and confusion or irritability can occur, almost always with one or more of the respiratory symptoms. In the early stages, it is difficult to distinguish COVID19 symptoms from other common viral infections. The most reliable diagnostic testing is detection of viral mRNA from a nasopharyngeal swab, using PCR assay, and rapid antigen tests (RATs).

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.2. Clinical features > 5.2. Clinical features
---
The incubation period is typically two to five days (up to 14 days). Unlike previous coronavirus outbreaks (e.g. SARS and MERS), transmission of SARS-CoV-2 can occur up to two days before the onset of symptoms[ 5 ] or from asymptomatic individuals[ 6 ]. Viral loads and infectivity are highest immediately after symptom onset, and most transmission occurs in household settings.[ 7 , 8 ]

Frequently, reinfection occurs due to exposure to variants of SARS-CoV-2 that are better able to evade the immune response and when inadequately matched neutralising antibody immunity has waned. The risk of reinfection is less in recently vaccinated individuals, and hybrid immunity, from both infection and vaccine, particularly reduces the risk of COVID-19 hospitalisation and death.[ 9 , 10 ] The degree of protection is highly variable and depends on individual risk for severe disease.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.2. Clinical features > 5.2. Clinical features
---
In children, the incidence of severe or fatal disease is significantly lower than in adults, with fewer hospitalisations and shorter hospital stays.11 Children at highest risk of more severe disease are those with pre-existing health conditions, particularly those with greater medical complexity.[ 11 ] These pre-existing conditions include immunocompromise, cardiovascular disorders, neurological and neurodevelopmental disorders (including Down syndrome / trisomy 21), chronic pulmonary diseases and type 1 diabetes.[ 12 ] Although some of these risk factors are prevalent in New Zealand children, particularly for some Māori and Pacific children,[ 13 , 14 ] during the first wave of sustained Omicron transmission in New Zealand, hospitalisations of predominantly COVID-19-na?ve children were of short duration (0-2 days) with few intensive care admissions.[ 15 ]

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.2. Clinical features > 5.2. Clinical features
---
Paediatric multisystem inflammatory syndrome temporally associated with SARS-CoV-2 (PIMS-TS or MIS-C) is a very rare, delayed complication of COVID-19 following largely asymptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection in children and adolescents.[ 16 , 17 ] During the first Omicron wave between January to June 2022, only 10 cases of the condition were seen in New Zealand in children aged under 16 years (incidence rate of 0.04 cases per 1,000 SARS-CoV-2 infections).[ 18 ] With most children having immunity to SARS-CoV-2 through previous infection and vaccination, the risk of PIMS-TS is now extremely low.[ 11 ]

section_heading: 5.2. Clinical features > 5.2.1. Groups at high risk of severe COVID-19
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.2. Clinical features > 5.2.1. Groups at high risk of severe COVID-19
---
As SARS-CoV-2 has evolved and people have developed hybrid immunity, from both vaccination and infection, the risk for severe disease has significantly reduced for the general population. Risk factors for severe disease are predominantly older age, immunodeficiencies and immunosuppression, and chronic cardiovascular, neurological or respiratory diseases.[ 19 ] Advanced age is the most important risk factor for severe disease, due to declining immune function and high prevalence of comorbidities. Even with a highly vaccinated population, the incidence of severe illness and mortality continues to be greatest in those aged over 70 years. Hospital admissions are around 12 and 25 times higher in those aged over 70 and over 80 years than those under 60 years (see Health New Zealand COVID-19 reporting .). Māori and Pacific populations experience age-related risk and increased frailty at a younger age than other ethnicities, increasing the risk for severe disease at a younger age than for other ethnicities.[ 20 ]

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.2. Clinical features > Pregnancy
---
With high vaccine uptake and widespread infection since 2022 in New Zealand, most people have reasonable immunity against SARS-CoV-2. Healthy individuals with immunity to SARS-CoV-2 face a lower risk of severe illness in pregnancy than non-immune individuals earlier in the pandemic. Despite this, some remain at increased risk for severe COVID-19 complications when pregnant, particularly when infection exacerbates underlying conditions or in high-risk pregnancy, for example those with gestational diabetes, hypertension, thrombosis risk factors, obesity or of an older age.[ 19 ]

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.2. Clinical features > Pregnancy
---
Although considerably lower than early in the pandemic and during the Delta variant period, pregnancy remained associated with an increased risk of moderate to severe COVID-19 complications when Omicron variants became predominant, particularly for unvaccinated people.[ 21 , 22 ] One international study found that the risk for referral to ICU or death was increased by 20 times among unvaccinated pregnant women with symptomatic Omicron COVID-19 in comparison to those without COVID-19.[ 23 ] Mothers with severe COVID-19 are at increased risk of preterm delivery, primarily due to early induction of labour.[ 24 , 25 ] Early studies did not detect intrauterine transmission, but perinatal transmission was shown in around 3 percent of neonates.[ 26 ] Most neonatal infections are asymptomatic or mild, but early data found around 12 percent experience severe disease, with dyspnoea and fever the most commonly reported signs.[ 27 ] Transfer of maternal antibody across the placenta provides passive protective antibody for newborn infants, which can be enhanced by a dose of COVID-19 vaccine

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.2. Clinical features > Pregnancy
---
assive protective antibody for newborn infants, which can be enhanced by a dose of COVID-19 vaccine in pregnancy.[ 26 , 28 ]

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.2. Clinical features > 5.2.2. Post-infection conditions
---
Post-acute COVID-19 sequelae, commonly called ‘long COVID’, is characterised by persistent symptoms lasting for more than three months and is an area of ongoing investigation. It has been particularly observed in those with at least five symptoms in the first week of illness or with comorbidities, female sex, older age, high body mass index and smoking.[ 29-31 ] Post-acute manifestations include cardiovascular, pulmonary and neurological effects, including chronic fatigue, dyspnoea, specific organ dysfunction and depression.[ 32 ] The risk of post-acute sequelae persists with reinfection.[ 33 ] Hybrid immunity, from infection and vaccination, has been shown to reduce the risk of long COVID-19 among healthcare workers, but it is unclear whether repeated vaccine doses add incrementally more protection against long COVID-19.[ 34 ]

For further information see the Health NZ website: info.health.nz/conditions-treatments/infectious-diseases/covid-19/long-covid/ (external link)

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.3. Epidemiology > 5.3.1. Global burden of disease
---
SARS-CoV-2 was first identified in January 2020 following clusters of distinctive pneumonia cases observed in Wuhan, China during December 2019. A public health emergency of international concern (PHEIC) was announced in late January 2020. By mid-March 2020, it had spread to over 114 countries and the global death toll surpassed one million by late September 2020. Case numbers and deaths continued to increase, with a rapid peak in cases at the end of December 2021 due to the more infectious Omicron variant. The PHEIC ended in May 2023 when WHO transitioned from the emergency response to longer-term sustained COVID-19 protection, control and management.[ 35 ]

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.3. Epidemiology > 5.3.1. Global burden of disease
---
On 26 October 2025, WHO reported over 7.1 million cumulative deaths due to COVID-19, but the total was likely to be considerably higher. The infection-fatality rate, while still high in older age groups, has reduced since the start of the pandemic due to the prevalent variants, population immunity, public health measures including vaccination, and improved clinical recognition and management. WHO data shows that 85 to 90 percent of those who have died due to COVID-19 since 2023 were aged 65 years and over.

See the WHO Coronavirus Disease (COVID19) Dashboard ( covid19.who.int (external link) ) for the latest official data. Actual rates are expected to be considerably higher than officially reported rates, since testing is rare, especially in mild and ambulatory cases.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.3. Epidemiology > 5.3.1. Global burden of disease
---
Globally, vaccines against COVID-19 are estimated to have averted around 2.4 million deaths (estimated range 1.4 – 4.0 million) between 2020 and 2024.[ 36 ] The first phase I clinical trial for a COVID-19 vaccine commenced in March 2020 and the first public vaccine dose was administered in the United Kingdom on 8 December 2020. By late 2022, 11 COVID-19 vaccines had been granted emergency-use listing or approval by the WHO.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.3. Epidemiology > 5.3.2. New Zealand epidemiology
---
The first case of COVID-19- was reported in New Zealand on 28 February 2020. Border restrictions were implemented on 16 March 2020, as cases numbers increased and clusters of transmission were identified. On 25 March 2020, New Zealand entered a nationwide lockdown (‘Alert level four’). With rapid contact tracing and the public health COVID-19 protection framework, the spread of SARS-CoV-2 was restricted during 2020 and 2021. Only 19 percent of the virus introductions in 2020 resulted in ongoing transmission or more than one additional case.[ 37 ] Prior to the outbreak of the Delta variant in August 2021, most of the reported cases during 2021 were imported from overseas (over 95 percent from 1 January to 9 August 2021).

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.3. Epidemiology > 5.3.2. New Zealand epidemiology
---
From 16 August 2021, the number of cases in New Zealand increased due to the highly virulent Delta variant. From January 2022, when a more infectious Omicron variant entered the community, case numbers rose sharply. However, at this stage around 90 percent of the population aged 12 years and over had been vaccinated with at least two doses of COVID-19 vaccine. By late February 2023, almost three years after the first reported case, there were over 2.2 million recorded cases, over 26,000 hospitalisations, and 711 ICU admissions for COVID-19. There were 1,599 deaths coded with COVID-19 as the underlying cause, and the vast majority (96 percent) were aged over 59 years.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.3. Epidemiology > 5.3.2. New Zealand epidemiology
---
Published in September 2022, the COVID-19 Mortality Report found that although COVID-19-attributed mortality was highest in older age groups, based on age-adjusted estimates, the risk of mortality under the age of 60 years was 3.7 times higher for those of Māori and 3.9 times higher for those of Pacific ethnicities than for European and Other ethnicities.[ 20] Comorbidity in those under 60 years increased the risk of mortality by 78 times and explained 59 percent of the increased risk for Māori and 69 percent for Pacific ethnicities. Vaccination was shown to have a strong protective effect: after adjusting for age, sex, comorbidities and vaccination status (at least two doses), mortality risk was lowered but still 1.7 times higher in Māori and 1.9 times higher for Pacific compared with European/Other ethnicities.[ 20 ] For the 2022 mortality report, see health.govt.nz/publications/covid-19-mortality-aotearoa-new-zealand-inequities-risk (external link) .

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.3. Epidemiology > 5.3.2. New Zealand epidemiology
---
During 2025, hospital admissions attributed to COVID-19 were 10- to 20-fold higher in those aged over 80 years and 70 – 79 years than any other age group: peaking in July 2025 at 2.5 per 100,000 and 1.2 per 100,000 population, respectively, compared with 0.1 per 100,000 for those aged under 60 years. For current details on case demographics see Health New Zealand COVID-19 reporting .

Emergence of new variants is monitored in New Zealand by PHF Science (formerly ESR) through whole genome sequencing of specimens taken from hospitalised cases and wastewater sampling (see the PHF Science digital library (external link) ).

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > 5.4.1. Introduction
---
During the initial rollout of COVID-19 vaccines, the New Zealand Medicines and Medical Devices Safety Authority (Medsafe) granted provisional consent for their use. This imposed restrictions on use of these vaccines by health professionals according to the available data at time of approval. Provisional approval status allowed New Zealanders early access to medicines with ‘significant unmet clinical need’ under the Medicines Act. From July 2024, COVID-19 vaccines were incorporated into Pharmaceutical Schedule. Safety of COVID-19 vaccines continues to be monitored by Medsafe, as for all vaccines and medicines, through the New Zealand pharmacovigilance system (see section 1.6 ).

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > Funded vaccines
---
The current COVID-19 vaccine (mRNA-CV, Comirnaty) consists of modified messenger ribonucleic acid (mRNA) encoding the full-length spike glycoprotein of the SARS-CoV-2 virus, inside a lipid nanoparticle. The spike protein mRNA has an adjuvant effect, so no additional adjuvant is included. The original version was designated BNT162b2 in clinical trials conducted by Pfizer and BioNTech. This mRNA vaccine delivers the instructions for human cells to build the viral antigen, SARS-CoV-2 spike protein. The modified mRNA is temporarily protected from degradation by the lipid nanoparticle that also facilitates fusion with the recipient’s cell wall.[ 38 , 39 ] The vaccine is updated periodically to target the most recent SARS-CoV-2 variants.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > mRNA-CV – Comirnaty (Pfizer/BioNTech)
---
mRNA-CV (30 μg) – for adults and adolescents aged 12 years and over Each 0.3 mL dose of mRNA-CV contains:

- 30 μg of a nucleoside-modified, single-stranded, 5’-capped mRNA encoding pre-fusion stabilised SARS-CoV-2 full-length, variant-matched spike glycoprotein embedded in a lipid nanoparticle. The mRNA is produced using cell-free in vitro transcription from DNA templates.

- The lipid nanoparticle contains ALC-0315 ((4 hydroxybutyl)azanediyl)bis(hexane-6,1-diyl)bis(2-hexyldecanoate)), ALC 0159 (2-[(polyethylene glycol)-2000]-N,N-ditetradecylacetamide), distearoylphosphatidylcholine (DSPC)) and cholesterol.

- Also contains Tris/sucrose buffer: tromethamine (also known as Tris), tromethamine hydrochloride, sucrose and water for injection.

mRNA-CV (10 μg), for children aged 5 to 11 years (blue cap) Each 0.3 mL dose contains:

- 10 μg of nucleoside-modified mRNA encoding variant-matched SARS-CoV-2 spike protein (as described for 30 μg formulation).

- lipid nanoparticle – as above

- Tris/sucrose buffer – as above.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > mRNA-CV – Comirnaty (Pfizer/BioNTech)
---
mRNA-CV (3 μg), for children aged 6 months to 4 years (yellow cap) Each 0.3 mL dose contains:

- 3 μg of nucleoside-modified mRNA encoding variant-matched SARS-CoV-2 spike protein (as described for 30 μg formulation)

- lipid nanoparticle – as above

- Tris/sucrose buffer – as above.

section_heading: 5.4. Vaccines > History of approved vaccines previously available in New Zealand
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > History of approved vaccines previously available in New Zealand
---
Clinical trials for COVID 19 vaccine candidates began shortly after the pandemic was announced in March 2020. Between October to December 2020, the New Zealand Government signed advanced purchase agreements for four vaccine candidates, with purchase dependent on approval for use from the New Zealand Medicines and Medical Devices Safety Authority (Medsafe). Vaccinations with the first COVID-19 vaccine (mRNA-CV) began in February 2021 in New Zealand. Formulations, for adults and children, have adapted with the evolving SARS-CoV-2 variants over time. See Appendix 1 for a history of the COVID-19 vaccines used in New Zealand.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > 5.4.3. Efficacy and effectiveness
---
This section focuses on data related to the only vaccine currently available in New Zealand, mRNA-CV (Comirnaty, Pfizer/BioNTech). Population-level immunity to COVID 19 has shifted significantly since 2020, with most people now having hybrid protection from both infection and repeated vaccination. This makes vaccine effectiveness estimates less precise and reduces the incremental benefit compared with the original clinical trials conducted in people na?ve to SARS CoV 2. Despite this, COVID-19 vaccination remains highly effective against severe disease and death, and in the short term may prevent symptomatic illness.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > 5.4.3. Efficacy and effectiveness
---
A decline in vaccine effectiveness was observed after the primary course against SARS-CoV-2 infection and mild disease, particularly with emerging Omicron variants, but protection against severe disease has been maintained and is enhanced with the use of additional doses.[ 40 , 41 ] Although neutralising antibody levels wane,[ 41 ] and lower levels are less effective against the Omicron variants, T cell responses and memory are maintained in vaccine recipients.[ 42 , 43 ] Many people now have hybrid immunity from both vaccination and infection. Across all age groups, at least three exposures to SARS-CoV-2 spike protein through vaccination and infection (hybrid immunity), provide significant protection against Omicron-associated hospitalisation and death. The greatest waning in immunity is observed in those aged over 65 years and adults with underlying medical conditions compared with healthy adults.[ 44 ] Further doses help to maintain a high level of immunity, particularly in the oldest or most frail adults and those with immunocompromise.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > Immunogenicity
---
SARS-CoV-2 variants continually evolve to be more immune evasive. High levels of neutralising antibody are required to prevent infection, which is particularly evident in individuals with a weaker response to vaccines, such asolder people and those with comorbidities that affect the immune response. This is circumvented in the short term by offering additional doses of vaccines that are adapted to better match circulating variants (eg, XBB.1.5, JN.1, LP.8.1 specific vaccines).

LP.8.1 variant-matched mRNA-CV, given to 42 health care workers in Germany, induced significant neutralisation antibody responses against pseudovirus particles with spike proteins of a range of variants: JN.1 (mean change 4.1-fold), LP.8.1 (8.1-fold), NB.1.8.1 (8.2-fold), XFG (15.0-fold) and BA.3.2.2 (2.1-fold, all p<0.0001). These data suggest that LP.8.1 mRNA-CV likely increases protection against severe COVID-19 caused by the circulating variants predominant in late 2025.[ 45 ]

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > Efficacy – clinical trial data
---
Early in the pandemic, efficacy studies were straightforward as most individuals were na?ve to SARS-CoV-2 and COVID-19 vaccines. Efficacy of mRNA-CV 30 μg (BNT162b2) was first assessed during 2020 in the phase III component of a large, clinical trial. A total of 43,448 participants aged 16–85 years across six countries were randomised to receive vaccine or saline placebo, with a primary series of two doses given 21 days apart. [ 46 ] Interim data, based on the early SARS-CoV-2 variants, indicated very high efficacy of 94.8 percent (95% CI: 89.8–97.6 percent) against symptomatic PCR-confirmed COVID-19.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > Efficacy – clinical trial data
---
igh efficacy of 94.8 percent (95% CI: 89.8–97.6 percent) against symptomatic PCR-confirmed COVID-19. Efficacy was consistent across all subgroups as defined by age, sex, race, ethnicity, baseline BMI and presence of co-existing conditions.[ 46 ] High efficacy of 100 percent (95% CI 75.3–100 percent) was reported in younger age groups (ages 12 –15 years) during 2020/2021.[ 47 ] In children aged 5 – 11 years, the efficacy of the lower dose formulation mRNA-CV (10μg) was 90.7 percent (95% CI 67.7–98.3) against symptomatic COVID-19.[ 48 ] Efficacy of mRNA-CV (3μg) was less well established in children aged under 5 years due to few cases. Combined for children aged 6 – 23 months and 2 – 4 years, vaccine efficacy of 80.4 percent (14.1–96.7 percent) against symptomatic infection was reported during a period of Omicron prevalence in the US.[ 49 , 50 ]

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > Efficacy – clinical trial data
---
As the pandemic progressed, hybrid immunity following both infection and vaccination developed, making placebo-controlled efficacy trials obsolete.

section_heading: 5.4. Vaccines > Effectiveness of primary course – early real-world experience
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > Effectiveness of primary course – early real-world experience
---
At the start of the COVID-19 immunisation programmes in late 2020/early 2021, mRNA-CV (30 μg) was highly effective at preventing severe COVID-19 and COVID-19-related death, in line with efficacy observed during clinical trials.[ 51 , 52 ] As the Delta variant emerged from mid-2021, mRNA-CV had lower effectiveness against symptomatic COVID-19 (ranging from around 78–93 percent), but the vaccine remained effective against hospitalisation (73–94 percent), and highly effective against severe disease and death (80–97 percent) in a range of groups.[ 52 ] Waning antibody levels were less effective against the Omicron variant and booster doses were required to help prevent symptomatic infection and reinfection (see below).

section_heading: 5.4. Vaccines > Effectiveness of primary course – early real-world experience
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > Effectiveness of primary course – early real-world experience
---
Vaccination with mRNA-CV in pregnancy reduces the risk of severe COVID-19 and provides passive immunity to the infant for the first few months of life.[ 53-55 ] A systematic review showed 70 percent reduction in SARS-CoV-2 infection in infants of vaccinated mothers for up to 6 months while Delta variant was prevalent; for the early Omicron variants, passive immunity effectiveness was less but the numbers of infants infected were very low.[ 56-58 ]

section_heading: 5.4. Vaccines > Duration of immunity and additional doses
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > Duration of immunity and additional doses
---
Despite hybrid immunity from both vaccination and infection, further vaccine doses are required to maintain a high level of immunity in elderly adults against emerging variants. A Canadian cohort study of people with previous exposure to Omicron (BA.1 and BA.2 predominance) estimated protection at one to two months after a fourth dose of vaccine to be 96 percent (95% CI 92-98).[ 59 ]

section_heading: 5.4. Vaccines > Duration of immunity and additional doses
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > Duration of immunity and additional doses
---
Additional doses with variant-matched vaccines (eg BA.4/5, XBB.1.5, JN.1 and LP.8.1) enhance protection against more immune-evasive Omicron variants.[ 42 , 60-62 ] Data from the Netherlands during October 2023 to January 2024 estimated incremental effectiveness of the XBB.1.5 mRNA-CV (30 μg) vaccine to be around 41 percent against Omicron infection for those aged 18 – 59 years and 50 percent for ages 60 – 85 years who had received at least one previous booster.[ 63 ] Additionally, XBB.1.5 mRNA-CV (30 μg) vaccine was around 70 percent effective against hospitalisation and ICU admission (95% CI 67–74 percent and 42–88 percent, respectively) within the first two months of vaccination in adults aged 60 years and over.[ 64 ] In the US, the overall vaccine effectiveness against symptomatic infection was 58 percent (48–65 percent) for up to 59 days after vaccination and 49 percent (36–58 percent) from 60 – 119 days after vaccination.[ 65 ] JN.1 variant-matched vaccine had a similar effectiveness profile against severe illness in older adults for at least 4

section_heading: 5.4. Vaccines > Duration of immunity and additional doses
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > Duration of immunity and additional doses
---
d vaccine had a similar effectiveness profile against severe illness in older adults for at least 4 months.[ 65 , 66 ]

section_heading: 5.4. Vaccines > mRNA COVID 19 vaccine – Comirnaty (30 μg)
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > mRNA COVID 19 vaccine – Comirnaty (30 μg)
---
The LP.8.1 variant-matched vaccine is presented in single-dose glass prefilled syringes. These are not stored in ultra-low temperatures prior to distribution. Store at +2°C to 8°C for up to 12 months. Do not freeze.

Transport according to the National Standards for Vaccine Storage and Transportation for Immunisation Providers 2017 (2nd edition) www.tewhatuora.govt.nz/health-services-and-programmes/vaccine-information/vaccine-service-delivery/cold-chain-standards-for-vaccines

section_heading: 5.4. Vaccines > mRNA COVID 19 vaccine – Comirnaty (3 μg and 10 μg)
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > mRNA COVID 19 vaccine – Comirnaty (3 μg and 10 μg)
---
These vaccines require storage at ultra-low temperatures (-90°C to -60°C) and at this temperature have a shelf-life of 18 months. Store unopened single (10μg) or multidose (3μg) vials at +2°C to 8°C for up to 10 weeks within the 18 months shelf-life. Do not freeze.

Transport according to the National Standards for Vaccine Storage and Transportation for Immunisation Providers 2017 (2nd edition) www.tewhatuora.govt.nz/health-services-and-programmes/vaccine-information/vaccine-service-delivery/cold-chain-standards-for-vaccines

For mRNA-CV (3μg), multidose vials require dilution prior to use (see Table 5.1). Once an undiluted vial is taken out of the refrigerator (fridge), allow a few minutes for the vaccine to reach room temperature prior to dilution. Store diluted vaccine in vials (ie punctured vial) at +2°C to 8°C for a maximum of 12 hours, or store vaccine drawn-up in syringe for a maximum of six hours at +2°C to 30°C. Discard any vaccine exceeding these times, accordingly.

section_heading: 5.4. Vaccines > mRNA-CV (30 μg) – for all ages from 12 years and over
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > mRNA-CV (30 μg) – for all ages from 12 years and over
---
Each dose of mRNA-CV (30 μg) is 0.3 mL to be administered intramuscularly.

One dose is given to individuals aged 12 years or older who have not received any previous COVID-19 vaccination. According to eligibility, additional doses are offered from six months after the first. This interval can be reduced to a minimum three months in certain circumstances. For additional dose eligibility see section 5.5.3 .

Each prefilled syringe contains 0.3 mL of vaccine to supply a single 0.3 mL dose. This vaccine is latex-free.

section_heading: 5.4. Vaccines > mRNA-CV (10 μg) for ages 5 to 11 years
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > mRNA-CV (10 μg) for ages 5 to 11 years
---
Each 0.3 ml dose mRNA-CV (10 μg) is to be administered intramuscularly. One dose is given to individuals aged 5 to less than 12 years.

Each single dose vial (with light blue cap) contains 0.48 mL of vaccine to supply a single 0.3 mL dose.

This vaccine is latex-free. The vial stopper is made with synthetic rubber (bromobutyl), not natural rubber latex.

section_heading: 5.4. Vaccines > mRNA-CV (3 μg) for ages 6 months – 4 years
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > mRNA-CV (3 μg) for ages 6 months – 4 years
---
Each 0.3 ml dose of mRNA-CV (3 μg) is to be administered intramuscularly. Three doses are given to individuals aged 6 months to less than 5 years. It is recommended to administer dose two at least three weeks after dose one followed by dose three at least eight weeks after dose two.

Each multidose vial (with a yellow cap) contains 0.48 ml of vaccine and should be diluted with 1.1 ml of 0.9 percent NaCl. Each reconstituted vial will supply three doses of 0.3 mL . If the amount of vaccine remaining in the vial cannot provide a full 0.3 mL dose, discard the vial and any excess volume. Do not pool excess vaccine from multiple vials.

This vaccine is latex-free. The vial stopper is made with synthetic rubber (bromobutyl), not natural rubber latex.

Preparing mRNA-CV multi-dose vial

section_heading: 5.4. Vaccines > mRNA-CV (3 μg) for ages 6 months – 4 years
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > mRNA-CV (3 μg) for ages 6 months – 4 years
---
Note that the process for drawing up mRNA-CV differs from the recommendations for other multi-dose vial vaccines as described in section A7.2 in Appendix 7 . To follow international guidance , the needle used to draw up mRNA-CV is also used to administer the injection to ensure accuracy of the 0.3ml dose.

For detailed instructions for mRNA-CV multi-dose vial preparation and administration see the most current IMAC Comirnaty factsheets available from immune.org.nz (external link) .

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > Coadministration with other vaccines
---
There are no anticipated safety concerns regarding coadministration any of the currently available COVID-19 vaccines with other vaccines. These vaccines can be administered, at any time before, after or simultaneously (in separate syringes, at separate sites), with other Schedule vaccines including PCV13, DTaP-IPV-HepB/Hib, DTaP-IPV, MMR, VV, influenza, HPV, Tdap and meningococcal vaccines.

For children aged 6 months – 4 years, it is recommended to give mRNA-CV (3 μg) in a different limb to MenB or MenACWY due to each vaccine’s reactogenicity. If feasible, spacing of at least three days between mRNA-CV and meningococcal vaccines will facilitate assessment of the reactogenicity of each vaccine. This is less important if antipyretic prophylaxis (eg paracetamol) is given as recommended to those aged under 2 years with MenB (see section 13.5.1 Usual childhood schedule).

TST/Mantoux testing for tuberculosis can be conducted at any time before, after or simultaneously with mRNA-CV.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.4. Vaccines > Coadministration with other vaccines
---
If the timing of mpox or COVID-19 vaccination is not urgent, consider spacing mRNA-CV and mpox vaccine 4 weeks apart. This is particularly relevant for young males and those who have a history of cardiac inflammation. While no link has been shown between this mpox vaccine and myocarditis,[ 67 ] this recommendation is in view of historical concerns of a potential link with older mpox/smallpox vaccines, and the rare but known link with mRNA COVID-19 vaccines.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule
---
From January 2022, as part of a special authority all individuals in New Zealand aged from 5 years were eligible to be vaccinated with a primary course. Vaccination was also introduced for certain high-risk infants aged from 6 months to 4 years, inclusively, in February 2023.

As the special authority has lapsed, COVID-19 vaccine (mRNA-CV) is now only funded for individuals eligible for publicly funded health and disability services, in accordance with the Pharmaceutical Schedule (external link) . See Table 5.2 and Table 5.3 for the recommended schedules.

Additional doses of age-appropriate, mRNA-CV (10 μg or 30 μg) are recommended for those aged 5 years and over who are at increased risk of severe COVID-19. An additional dose can be given at least six months after a previous dose of COVID-19 vaccine. See Table 5.2 and Table 5.2 for recommended spacing. All other adults aged 30 years and over are also eligible for additional doses of mRNA-CV (30μg) if requested, every six months.

Additional doses are only funded for this age group if they have certain underlying medical conditions (see Table 5.3 and section 5.5.3 ). Healthy frontline healthcare, aged-care or disability workers aged under 30 years are not eligible for additional doses.

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > mRNA-CV (30 μg) for all ages from 12 years
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > mRNA-CV (30 μg) for all ages from 12 years
---
All individuals from the age of 12 years who have not previously received any doses of COVID-19 vaccine are recommended to receive a single dose of mRNA-CV (30 μg). For an additional primary dose for severely immunocompromised, see section 5.5.2 .

Individuals aged from 16 years can be offered a further single dose, after receiving primary course of any COVID-19 vaccine, given at least three months later. Additional doses are not generally recommended for healthy individuals age 16 – 29 years. See Table 5.3 and section 5.5.3 for additional dose recommendations for groups at higher risk from severe COVID-19.

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > mRNA-CV (10 μg) for ages 5 to 11 years (light blue cap)
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > mRNA-CV (10 μg) for ages 5 to 11 years (light blue cap)
---
Individuals from the age of 5 years who have not previously received any doses of COVID-19 vaccine can have a single dose of mRNA-CV (10 μg). See section 5.5.2 for two-dose primary course recommendations for those who are severely immunocompromised. See Table 5.3 and section 5.5.3 for additional dose recommendations for high-risk groups.

For children who are severely immunocompromised and eligible for two primary doses (as described in section 5.5.2 ), who turn 12 years after their first dose, continue with the same dose of paediatric vaccine (ie, mRNA-CV (10 μg)) for the subsequent dose, if it is less than three months since previous dose. If it is longer than 3 months, give age-appropriate vaccine (ie. mRNA-CV (30μg)). Give an additional dose of the current variant-matched mRNA-CV (30μg) at least 6 months later (for additional doses see Table 5.3 and section 5.5.3 ).

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > mRNA-CV (3 μg) for ages 6 months to 4 years (yellow cap)
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > mRNA-CV (3 μg) for ages 6 months to 4 years (yellow cap)
---
Three doses of a paediatric formulation, mRNA-CV (3μg), are available for certain children aged 6 months to 4 years. Dose two is administered at least three weeks after dose one followed by dose three at least eight weeks after dose two.

The use of this vaccine is limited to young children who are at highest risk of severe disease if they were to catch COVID-19 such as those with severe immunocompromise or with complex and/or multiple health conditions (see section 5.5.4 and the starship.org.nz/guidelines/covid-19-disease-in-children/ (external link) ).

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > mRNA-CV (3 μg) for ages 6 months to 4 years (yellow cap)
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > mRNA-CV (3 μg) for ages 6 months to 4 years (yellow cap)
---
Children who started the three-dose primary course with a previous mRNA-CV (3 μg) vaccine formulation can complete the three-dose series with the current variant-matched mRNA-CV (3 μg). Children who start their primary course when aged under 5 years need three doses, even if they turn 5 years part way through the course. For children who turn 5 years after their first or second dose, continue with the same dose of paediatric vaccine (ie, mRNA-CV (3 μg)) for subsequent primary doses, if it is less than three months since previous dose: dose two is given at least three weeks after first dose and dose three is given at least 8 weeks after previous dose. If it is longer than 3 months between their primary doses, give an age-appropriate vaccine (ie. mRNA-CV (10μg)).

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.2. Severely immunocompromised individuals aged 5 years and over
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.2. Severely immunocompromised individuals aged 5 years and over
---
There are no safety concerns around administering mRNA-CV to individuals who are immunocompromised and/or receiving immunosuppressive agents. As with other non-live vaccines, the antibody response to this vaccine may be reduced and protection could be suboptimal or wane quickly but is likely to be adequate to protect against severe disease.

A second (extra) primary dose of mRNA-CV (10 μg or 30 μg, as age-appropriate) is indicated for certain individuals aged 5 years and over who are severely immunocompromised and are unlikely to respond adequately to a single primary dose (for children younger than 5 years, see 5.5.4 ). Serology is not recommended. A second primary dose is distinct from additional doses (see section 5.5.3 ). Preferably, dose two of a primary course is given eight weeks after dose one.

For examples, see the IMAC factsheet COVID-19 vaccinations for individuals with severe immunocompromise . ( immune.org.nz (external link) )

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.2. Severely immunocompromised individuals aged 5 years and over > Timing of vaccinations
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.2. Severely immunocompromised individuals aged 5 years and over > Timing of vaccinations
---
It is recommended to discuss the optimal timing for vaccination with a specialist before the vaccine appointment for those who are severely immunocompromised. Ideally, vaccination should be conducted prior to any planned immunosuppression (see section 4.3.6 ). When offering vaccination consider current or planned immunosuppressive therapies. Where possible, vaccinate during a treatment ‘holiday’ or at the lowest point of immunosuppression between doses of treatment.

For children who turn 12 years after their first dose, continue with the same dose of paediatric vaccine (ie, mRNA-CV (10 μg)) for the subsequent dose, if it is less than three months since the first dose. If the spacing is greater than 3 months, complete the primary course with an age-appropriate vaccine (ie mRNA-CV (30 μg). Give an additional dose of mRNA-CV (30μg) 6 months later with the most current variant-matched vaccine.

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.2. Severely immunocompromised individuals aged 5 years and over > Timing of vaccinations
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.2. Severely immunocompromised individuals aged 5 years and over > Timing of vaccinations
---
If a significant adverse reaction to mRNA-CV has occurred, it is recommended to seek advice from IMAC to determine whether further doses are contraindicated.

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.3. Additional doses for ages 5 years and older after primary course
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.3. Additional doses for ages 5 years and older after primary course
---
NB: Certain individuals with severe immunocompromise are recommended to receive two primary doses (see section 5.5.2 ; this is part of the primary course and not the same as additional doses described in this section). Following the two-dose primary course, these individuals are also eligible for additional doses as described here.

To maintain effective neutralising antibody levels, vaccination with the most current variant-matched COVID-19 vaccine is particularly recommended for high-risk groups.

Additional doses of age-appropriate, mRNA-CV (10 μg or 30 μg) are recommended for those aged 5 years and over who are at increased risk of severe COVID-19. An additional dose can be given at least six months after a previous dose of COVID-19 vaccine. See Table 5.2 and Table 5.3 for recommended spacing. All other adults aged 30 years and over are also eligible for additional doses of mRNA-CV (30μg) if requested, every six months.

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.3. Additional doses for ages 5 years and older after primary course
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.3. Additional doses for ages 5 years and older after primary course
---
Clinical discretion can be applied, case by case, when considering vaccination given less than six months after a previous dose. A shorter spacing of a minimum of three months after a previous vaccination may be appropriate for those individuals considered to be at very high risk of severe disease from COVID-19 infection, for operational requirements (eg. aged-care residential facilities), or when a newer variant-matched vaccine becomes available and there is urgency to vaccinate those at very high risk. Spacing of either at least six months or 12 months is preferred, depending on risk for severe COVID-19.

In addition, anyone aged 16 years or over who has not yet received an additional dose since completion of the primary course is eligible for a single dose of mRNA-CV (30μg), if not contraindicated. Although, with the likelihood of repeated exposure to SARS-CoV-2 since completion of the primary course, this is not generally required.

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.3. Additional doses for ages 5 years and older after primary course
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.3. Additional doses for ages 5 years and older after primary course
---
See the IMAC factsheet for examples of groups at higher risk of severe COVID-19 that are not severely immunocompromised ( immune.org.nz (external link) ).

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > Timing of additional doses
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > Timing of additional doses
---
Due to rapidly waning immunity in those less able to mount a strong immune response, such as older adults and those with immunocompromise, individuals with an increased risk from severe breakthrough COVID-19 are particularly recommended to have a dose of the most current variant-matched mRNA-CV (30 μg or 10 μg, as age-appropriate) and continue boosting twice yearly. Other groups at risk from COVID-19 complications with less pronounced waning immunity are recommended annual doses of mRNA-CV.

Twice yearly vaccination (ie given every 6 months, as practicable) is recommended for:

- All adults aged 75 years over

- people living in residential care facilities aged from 65 years

- severely immunocompromised people aged 5 years and over (see section 5.5.2 )

- individuals aged from 50 years with pronounced frailty / multiple comorbidities, based on clinical discretion

Once yearly (ie given every 12 months) is recommended for:

- All aged 65 to 74 years

- All Māori and Pacific people aged 50 to 74 years

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > Timing of additional doses
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > Timing of additional doses
---
- Living in residential care facilities aged 16 to 64 years

- Anyone aged 5 years and over with one or more comorbidity that increases risk for severe COVID-19 infection, including: Cardiovascular diseases (excluding hypertension or dyslipidaemia without evidence of end-organ disease) chronic respiratory disease diabetes mellitus [requiring medication] chronic liver failure chronic kidney disease stage 4-5 any active cancer (excluding basal and squamous skin cancer if not invasive) other conditions with overall impact on health (assess level of immunocompromise and risk of severe disease to make clinical judgement about spacing between additional doses)

- Cardiovascular diseases (excluding hypertension or dyslipidaemia without evidence of end-organ disease)

- chronic respiratory disease

- diabetes mellitus [requiring medication]

- chronic liver failure

- chronic kidney disease stage 4-5

- any active cancer (excluding basal and squamous skin cancer if not invasive)

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > Timing of additional doses
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > Timing of additional doses
---
- other conditions with overall impact on health (assess level of immunocompromise and risk of severe disease to make clinical judgement about spacing between additional doses)

- Pregnancy recommended for those with comorbidities that increase the risk for COVID-19 or deemed high-risk pregnancy. (see Pregnancy section 5.5.6 ) all pregnant women can receive a dose of mRNA-CV, if requested, at least 12 months since a previous dose.

- recommended for those with comorbidities that increase the risk for COVID-19 or deemed high-risk pregnancy. (see Pregnancy section 5.5.6 )

- all pregnant women can receive a dose of mRNA-CV, if requested, at least 12 months since a previous dose.

- Anyone aged 30 years and over Carers, frontline healthcare, aged-care or disability workers from aged 30 years and over can receive one dose mRNA-CV annually, with a priority for those working with frail elderly or severely immunocompromised. Healthy individuals are not routinely recommended an additional dose, but it is available on request.

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > Timing of additional doses
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > Timing of additional doses
---
- Carers, frontline healthcare, aged-care or disability workers from aged 30 years and over can receive one dose mRNA-CV annually, with a priority for those working with frail elderly or severely immunocompromised.

- Healthy individuals are not routinely recommended an additional dose, but it is available on request.

These examples are not exhaustive, and providers may include individuals with conditions similar to those listed above, based on clinical judgement. See the IMAC factsheet for examples of special groups recommended for ongoing COVID-19 vaccinations (excluding severely immunocompromised) ( immune.org.nz (external link) ).

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.4. Children aged 6 months to 4 years with severe immunocompromise, complex or multiple health conditions.
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.4. Children aged 6 months to 4 years with severe immunocompromise, complex or multiple health conditions.
---
For children aged 6 months to 4 years, three primary doses of mRNA-CV (3 μg) are available for those who are severely immunocompromised, and for children who have complex or multiple health conditions that increase their risk from COVID-19. These include:

- chronic lung disease including bronchiectasis, cystic fibrosis, BiPAP for obstructive sleep apnoea (not asthma)

- complex congenital heart disease, acquired heart disease or congestive heart failure

- diabetes (insulin-dependent)

- chronic kidney disease (GFR <15 ml/min/1.73m2)

- severe cerebral palsy or severe neurodisability (including neuromuscular disorders)

- complex genetic, liver, metabolic disease or multiple congenital anomalies, including trisomy 21/Down Syndrome

- primary or acquired immunodeficiency, including HIV infection

- haematological malignancy and/or post-transplant (solid organ or HSCT in last 24 months)

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.4. Children aged 6 months to 4 years with severe immunocompromise, complex or multiple health conditions.
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.4. Children aged 6 months to 4 years with severe immunocompromise, complex or multiple health conditions.
---
- on immunosuppressive treatment including chemotherapy, high-dose corticosteroids, biologic or disease-modifying agents (see section 5.5.2 for considerations around timing of vaccinations)

For further details see the starship.org.nz/guidelines/covid-19-disease-in-children/ (external link) and the IMAC factsheet on COVID-19 vaccinations for individuals with severe immunocompromise at immune.org.nz (external link) .

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > Additional doses for children aged 6 months to 4 years
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > Additional doses for children aged 6 months to 4 years
---
An additional dose of the most current variant-matched mRNA-CV (3 μg) can be given to children aged from 6 months to 4 years who have received a three-dose primary series (as above) who are either severely immunocompromised (6 monthly) or who have complex or multiple health conditions that increase their risk from COVID-19 (annually). With clinical discretion, to facilitate vaccination around treatment, spacing can be reduced to at least three months. See Table 5.3 for recommended spacing and section 5.5.2 for timing considerations around treatments.

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.5. Vaccination following SARS-CoV-2 infection
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.5. Vaccination following SARS-CoV-2 infection
---
Vaccination should be offered regardless of an individual’s history of symptomatic or asymptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection. Hybrid immunity from at least three exposures to both vaccination and infection provides longer lasting protection against hospitalisation and death than vaccine or infection alone.[ 59 ] Although, there are no specific safety concerns around giving mRNA-CV to individuals with a history of symptomatic COVID-19 or asymptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection, those who have had recent infection may experience more systemic reactogenicity after mRNA-CV (see section 5.7.1 ).[ 68 ] Viral or serological testing is not required before vaccination.

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.5. Vaccination following SARS-CoV-2 infection
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.5. Vaccination following SARS-CoV-2 infection
---
For most healthy, previously vaccinated people under the age of 50 years, SARS-CoV-2 infection can boost their immunity to a level like that following a dose of vaccine and are unlikely to require further vaccine doses for at least 12 months after infection.[ 69 ] However, most people do not know they have had COVID-19 , so it is generally advised for individuals at increased risk of severe disease, to receive recommended doses regardless of recent COVID-19 infection, when no longer acutely unwell.

For all other vaccines, vaccination can also commence as soon as the individual is no longer acutely unwell.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.6. Pregnancy
---
Pregnancy can increase the risk of an adverse outcomes from COVID-19 infection, particularly for those with underlying conditions, when compared to age-matched non-pregnant adults (see section 5.2.1 ).[ 24 ]

mRNA-CV (30 μg) vaccine can be used in pregnancy and while breastfeeding.

- If previously unvaccinated, a single primary dose of mRNA-CV (30 μg) can be given at any stage during the pregnancy.

- For those who have been previously immunised, a dose of mRNA-CV (30 μg) is recommended during pregnancy for those who are at increased risk from severe COVID-19. This includes anyone who is pregnant with: a high-risk pregnancy (e.g. gestation diabetes, hypertension, thrombosis, obesity with BMI ≥40 kg/m2 or with a history of pregnancy complications) with underlying medical conditions or meets other eligibility criteria (given above in section 5.5.3 ) of older age (usually considered older for pregnancy in those aged 35 years and older).

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.6. Pregnancy
---
- a high-risk pregnancy (e.g. gestation diabetes, hypertension, thrombosis, obesity with BMI ≥40 kg/m2 or with a history of pregnancy complications)

- with underlying medical conditions or meets other eligibility criteria (given above in section 5.5.3 )

- of older age (usually considered older for pregnancy in those aged 35 years and older).

- Anyone who is pregnant is eligible and can choose to receive a dose of mRNA-CV at any stage of pregnancy, given from 12 months after a previous COVID-19 vaccination.

- mRNA-CV can be given at the same time as influenza vaccine and pertussis (Tdap) vaccine, or separately.

People who are trying to become pregnant do not need to avoid pregnancy after receiving mRNA-CV.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.6. Pregnancy
---
International evidence from large quantities of safety surveillance has found no safety concerns with administering mRNA-CV in any stage of pregnancy for the mother or her infant. [ 70-74 ] Transfer of maternal antibody across the placenta and in breastmilk provides passive protection to infants against hospitalisation for up to six months when enhanced by a COVID-19 vaccination in pregnancy.[ 26, 28, 55, 75-77 ]

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.6. Pregnancy
---
Although there is limited data available yet for the use of the LP.8.1 mRNA-CV (30 μg) formulation in pregnancy, the safety of mRNA-CV in pregnancy is inferred from the large quantity of reassuring data from the use of the original and other variant-matched mRNA-CVs given during pregnancy. Observational data for these vaccines show no increased risk of adverse pregnancy outcomes or increased risk of miscarriage in first trimester. The safety profile of additional doses of original mRNA COVID-19 vaccines given in pregnancy is as seen with the primary course.[ 78 ] During use in clinical trials and in real-world use from the age of 12 years, no clinically meaningful difference in safety profiles has been shown between the mRNA COVID-19 vaccines. There is no theoretically plausible reason for there to be any increased risk in pregnancy because the differences between these vaccine formulations are confined to mRNA spike protein sequences.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.7. Breastfeeding
---
As with all schedule vaccines, there are no safety concerns about giving mRNA-CV to those who are lactating.

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.8. Individuals receiving cardiology care
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.8. Individuals receiving cardiology care
---
It is recommended for all previously unvaccinated individuals from age 5 years to receive one dose of mRNA-CV (30 μg or 10 μg, as age appropriate). Pre-existing cardiac conditions, in general, are not regarded as precautions or contraindications to vaccination. This includes pre-existing rheumatic heart disease. Note that many cardiac conditions increase the risk from COVID-19 infection.

Individuals aged from 5 years with a history of pericarditis or myocarditis, unrelated to mRNA-CV, can have the vaccine once the condition is completely resolved, (ie, no symptoms and no evidence of ongoing cardiac inflammation).

section_heading: 5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.8. Individuals receiving cardiology care
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.8. Individuals receiving cardiology care
---
Infants and young children aged from 6 months to 4 years with complex congenital heart disease, acquired heart disease or congestive heart failure are recommended to received three doses of mRNA-CV (3 μg), dose two given at least three weeks after dose one and dose three given at least eight weeks later. An additional dose can be considered at 6 months after the previous vaccine dose or SARS-CoV-2 infection. For young children with a history of inflammatory heart disease, discuss with cardiologist/specialist paediatrician.

For those with a history of myocarditis and pericarditis related to mRNA-CV, seek specialist immunisation advice on a case-by-case basis to consider appropriate vaccination options and timing of any further doses. See section 5.6.2 for those who have myocarditis associated with mRNA-CV.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.5. Recommended immunisation schedule > 5.5.9. Revaccination
---
Individuals from age 6 months who have undergone haematopoietic stem cell transplantation since their first course can be revaccinated with a two or three-dose primary course of mRNA-CV (as age appropriate, see section 5.5.2 and 5.5.4 ), plus additional doses of mRNA-CV spaced as age appropriate (see Table 5.3 ).

Based on clinical discretion, if all scheduled doses have been completed prior to commencement of chemotherapy or solid organ transplant, a single further dose of mRNA-CV can be given, and continued as appropriate for age and level of immunosuppression (see Table 5.3) once or twice yearly (ie 6 or 12 months apart) with the most current variant-matched vaccine.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.6. Contraindications and precautions
---
See also section ?2.1.3 for pre-vaccination screening guidelines and section ?2.1.4 for general contraindications for all vaccines.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.6. Contraindications and precautions > 5.6.1. Contraindications
---
Vaccination with mRNA-CV is contraindicated for individuals with a history of anaphylaxis to any component or previous dose the same vaccine.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.6. Contraindications and precautions > 5.6.2. Precautions
---
A definite history of immediate allergic reaction to any other product is considered as a precaution but not a contraindication to vaccination with COVID-19 vaccines (mRNA-CV). A slightly increased risk of a severe allergic response in individuals who have had a previous anaphylaxis-type reaction needs to be balanced against the risk of SARS-CoV-2 exposure and severe COVID 19. These individuals can still receive COVID-19 vaccines, if not contraindicated, and observation extended to 30 minutes after vaccination in health care settings, where anaphylaxis can be immediately treated with adrenaline.

section_heading: 5.6. Contraindications and precautions > Myocarditis or pericarditis
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.6. Contraindications and precautions > Myocarditis or pericarditis
---
If myocarditis, myopericarditis or pericarditis occurs after a dose of COVID-19 vaccine (mRNA-CV or another COVID-19 vaccine) defer further doses of COVID-19 vaccination. Seek specialist immunisation advice, on a case-by-case basis, to consider timing for further doses, an appropriate alternative vaccine or no further vaccination. Vaccination is not recommended for anyone with current active cardiac inflammation.

The risk of myocarditis following vaccination is not thought to be greater in children aged 6 months to 4 years than any other group, acknowledging that background rates of myocarditis from any cause in infants (aged under 1 year) are generally higher than in older children. There is no current evidence of a safety concern with this vaccine in young children or infants, overall.

section_heading: 5.7. Potential responses and AEFIs > 5.7.1. Potential responses
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.7. Potential responses and AEFIs > 5.7.1. Potential responses
---
Commonly reported responses to mRNA-CV (30 μg) are injection-site pain, headache, dizziness and fatigue; other responses included muscle aches, feeling generally unwell, chills, fever, chest discomfort, joint pain, nausea and axillar lymph node swelling. These occurred most often after dose two and in younger adults (aged 18–55 years), and within one or two days of vaccination. Most are mild or moderate in severity and are self-limiting. [ 46, 75 ] According to Australian AusVaxSafety active surveillance data, the responses to XBB.1.5 and JN.1 variant-matched mRNA-CVs, given as a single primary dose or as an additional doses, were similar or milder than the previous mRNA-CV vaccines.[ 79, 80 ] No new adverse reactions have been identified in clinical trials and real-world usage. [ 80 ] Analgesia, such as paracetamol or ibuprofen (as appropriate), can be taken for pain and discomfort following vaccination. It is advisable to limit vigorous exercise if feeling unwell.

section_heading: 5.7. Potential responses and AEFIs > 5.7.1. Potential responses
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.7. Potential responses and AEFIs > 5.7.1. Potential responses
---
Responses to the paediatric formulation mRNA-CV (10 μg) in children aged 5–11 years are similar to those seen for the adult formulation mRNA-CV (30 μg) in those age 16–25 years. During clinical trials, reactions were generally mild to moderate and short-lived. Pain at injection site was commonly reported (by over 70 percent) after dose one and two. Overall fewer children reported systemic reactions than adults, with fever, fatigue, headache, chills and muscle ache as the most common and more frequent after the second dose. [ 48 ] These responses were mirrored in reports to the Vaccine Adverse Event Reporting System (VAERS) and V-safe after 8.7 million doses given routinely to children in the US. [ 81 ] As with adults, the responses to paediatric variant-matched mRNA-CV, given as a single primary dose or as an additional dose, are expected to be similar or milder than the earlier mRNA-CVs.

section_heading: 5.7. Potential responses and AEFIs > 5.7.1. Potential responses
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.7. Potential responses and AEFIs > 5.7.1. Potential responses
---
In clinical trial for paediatric mRNA-CV (3 μg), the most frequent responses seen in infants (aged 6–23 months) were irritability, decreased appetite, injection-site tenderness and redness, and fever; and in children aged 2–4 years, injection-site pain and redness, fatigue and fever. Less common responses included lymphadenopathy, diarrhoea, vomiting and nausea. Consistent with the clinical trial, systemic reactions were more frequently reported to V-safe in the US for infants (ages 6 months – 2 years) than those aged 3–5 years. [ 82 ]

See chapter 2 ( section 2.3.3 ) for immunisation-stress related responses (ISRR).

section_heading: 5.7. Potential responses and AEFIs > Breast screening and CT scans
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.7. Potential responses and AEFIs > Breast screening and CT scans
---
Transient unilateral axillary adenopathy, a known response to vaccination, was particularly noted following vaccination with mRNA-CV due to the scale of the roll-out and age groups being immunised. Early estimates suggest that 12–16 percent of vaccine recipients experience axillary adenopathy after vaccination with mRNA-CV, starting one or two days after vaccination and which can persist for several weeks.[ 81, 83, 84 ] Lymphadenopathy has also been commonly reported after additional doses of mRNA-CV. [ 85 ]

When attending breast screening appointments, including MRI scans and mammography, it is recommended that individuals advise the radiographer or doctor that they have received a COVID-19 vaccine recently. It is advised to monitor any lymph node changes that persist for longer than six weeks after vaccination. [ 83 ]

section_heading: 5.7. Potential responses and AEFIs > Breast screening and CT scans
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.7. Potential responses and AEFIs > Breast screening and CT scans
---
Likewise, individuals undergoing FDG PET/CT or MRI scans for cancer screening are advised to inform the radiologist or their oncologist that they have been recently vaccinated, or, if possible, to have COVID-19 vaccination at least two weeks before a scheduled scan or as soon as possible afterwards. Treatment should not be delayed.

页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.7. Potential responses and AEFIs > 5.7.2. AEFIs
---
Adverse events following immunisation (AEFIs) with the COVID-19 vaccines continue to be monitored, as for all vaccines and medicines, as part of the New Zealand and global pharmacovigilance systems.

A list of adverse events of special interest (AESIs), including those previously associated with immunisation in general and with the individual vaccine platforms, was created by Safety Platform for Emergency Vaccines (SPEAC) in collaboration with the Coalition for Epidemic Preparedness Innovations (CEPI) and based on existing and new Brighton Collaboration case definitions. For further information, see brightoncollaboration.us/covid-19 (external link) . Global pharmacovigilance and active safety monitoring systems continue to watch for both AESI and unexpected AEFI.

section_heading: 5.7. Potential responses and AEFIs > Early clinical trials and safety surveillance
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.7. Potential responses and AEFIs > Early clinical trials and safety surveillance
---
In adults and children aged 12 years and over, preliminary phase II/III clinical trial safety data reported lymphadenopathy in 64 (0.3%) vaccine recipients and six (<0.1%) placebo recipients (follow-up of up to 14 weeks after second dose of a subset of 18,860 participants who received at least one dose of mRNA-CV). Four vaccine-related adverse events were recorded: namely, shoulder injury related to vaccine administration; lymphadenopathy local to injection site; paroxysmal ventricular arrhythmia; and right leg paraesthesia. No deaths were related to either the vaccine or the placebo.[ 46 ] During clinical trial follow-up to 1 February 2021, acute peripheral facial paralysis (Bell’s palsy) was reported by four vaccinated participants and none in the placebo group. [ 86 ] Subsequent analyses found no detected safety signal for this condition as an AESI, [ 87 ] and safety monitoring is ongoing.

section_heading: 5.7. Potential responses and AEFIs > Early clinical trials and safety surveillance
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.7. Potential responses and AEFIs > Early clinical trials and safety surveillance
---
In children aged under 12 years, no vaccine-related severe adverse events were seen during the phase II/III clinical trial of mRNA-CV (10 μg) and mRNA-CV (3 μg). Lymphadenopathy was reported in ten (0.9 percent) children out of 1,518 recipients aged 5–11 years who received of mRNA-CV (10 μg). Four participants had mild and self-limiting rashes with no consistent pattern and with typical onset seven or more days after vaccination, which were considered related to vaccination. No differences were apparent in vaccine safety between the children who had baseline evidence of previous SARS-CoV-2 infection. [ 48 ] Following administration of approximately 8.7 million doses of mRNA-CV (10 μg) in children aged 5–11 years in the US, the majority of reports to VAERS were non-serious (97.6 percent) and 2.4 percent were serious. The most common non-serious reports were due to vaccine administration errors.

section_heading: 5.7. Potential responses and AEFIs > Early clinical trials and safety surveillance
content:
页面标题：5. Coronavirus disease (COVID-19)
章节：5.7. Potential responses and AEFIs > Early clinical trials and safety surveillance
---
